quote: Sometimes it just it's a little it doesn't stick.
stewart_read: LaFalla's mild frustration at 01:00 ('Didn't I put that on the phone?') was the earliest recoverable friction point in the call. The rep's vague deflection — 'it doesn't stick' — is the kind of non-answer that reads as evasive to a skeptical customer. The codex's honest-specificity principle applies here: a brief, direct acknowledgment ('The form captures the basics, but I need to confirm a couple of things live to make sure the design is accurate for your home') would have validated LaFalla's frustration and given him a reason for the call. Instead, the vague deflection primed the harder channel-redirect that followed 7 seconds later.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep was moving quickly to avoid dwelling on the form-data gap. Rejected — there is no evidence of a deliberate pivot strategy, and the vagueness produced the opposite of the intended effect.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky this 7-second window. The channel-redirect at 01:10 did not come from nowhere — it was primed here. Drill the honest-specificity response: one sentence that validates the form submission and gives a concrete reason for the live questions. This is the earliest coaching moment in the call.

quote: Don't bother me anymore on the phone. Send me an email or send a hard copy of how your company can help me with my, electric bill.
stewart_read: LaFalla's channel-redirect is a contact-preference complaint, not a cost or interest objection. The correct move is to acknowledge the preference, then reframe: the appointment IS the written deliverable he wants — it produces a custom design with real numbers he can compare against his two existing quotes. The rep instead counter-pushed immediately ('Yeah. I can help you right now'), which read as dismissive of a stated preference. Note: the codex flags protocols.excessive_contact_complaint as TBD — this call is a clean source-of-truth candidate for populating that section.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep was trying to hold the call by demonstrating immediate value. Rejected — the counter-push had no acknowledgment component, so it landed as resistance rather than helpfulness.
coaching_implication: This is the call's primary coaching moment. Walk Bucky through the two-step: (1) acknowledge the preference explicitly ('Totally fair — I won't keep you on the phone'), then (2) reframe the appointment as the vehicle that produces what LaFalla actually wants ('The way we get you something to compare against those two quotes is a 30-minute call with our specialist — they build the design live and you walk away with real numbers'). The reframe does not promise to send materials pre-appointment, so it stays within the send_proposal_before_meeting hard rule.

quote: Oh, so you haven't gotten enough information about me to to get to send you a quote? I want to send you a quote because I already have quotes from two other companies.
stewart_read: LaFalla reveals he has two competing quotes and is actively seeking a third — this is a live competitive opening, not a disqualifier. The codex's already_saw_design rebuttal applies: 'It's always best to get a couple looks so you have something to compare against. We offer different equipment than most companies and our price points can be different.' The rep did not deploy this or any variant. Instead, the rep was already in a defensive posture from the channel-redirect exchange and had no bandwidth to recognize the competitive signal as an opportunity.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to show Bucky what a competitive signal looks like when it's buried inside a complaint. LaFalla is not saying 'I don't want solar' — he is saying 'I want a third quote and I want it in writing.' That is a qualified, motivated customer. The already_saw_design rebuttal is the bridge. Drill the recognition pattern: customer mentions existing quotes → that is an invitation, not a rejection.

quote: I want you to send me an email. List all of the ways that your company can help me lower my electricity bill or send me a hard copy in the mail.
stewart_read: LaFalla's second channel-redirect is more specific — he wants a list of ways Ion can help, delivered in writing. The rep's response ('everything we do is specific to homes') is procedurally accurate but lands as a refusal without an alternative. The send_proposal_before_meeting hard rule prohibits sending the proposal pre-appointment, but it does not prohibit acknowledging the preference and bridging to the appointment as the mechanism that produces the written output. The rep treated the hard rule as a conversation-ender rather than a constraint to work within.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep was correctly holding the hard rule. Partially supported — the rule was held, but the execution produced a dead end rather than a redirect. Holding a hard rule and losing the customer is not a success.
coaching_implication: Distinguish between holding the rule and holding the customer. The rule says don't send the proposal before the meeting. It does not say refuse to engage with the customer's preference. Drill the bridge: 'I can't send a generic list because what we can do for you depends entirely on your home — that's exactly what the 30-minute call produces. You walk away with a custom design you can put next to your other two quotes.' Rule held, door open.

quote: Okay. That's fine. Yeah. I literally just have, like, three other questions, and then it's that's it.
stewart_read: After LaFalla reveals two competing quotes, the rep's response is to push back to the verify checklist ('I literally just have, like, three other questions'). This is the wrong pivot — LaFalla has just signaled he is in the market and wants a deliverable. The rep's focus on completing the verify sequence rather than addressing the competitive signal and the channel preference shows a procedural orientation that is mismatched to where the customer actually is in the conversation.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep was trying to complete qualification before pivoting to the appointment ask. Rejected — LaFalla had already issued two channel-redirect directives; returning to the checklist without first resolving the underlying preference reads as tone-deaf.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky the sequence: LaFalla gave two competitive signals (existing quotes, wants a written deliverable) and the rep responded by asking for more form data. The verify sequence is a tool, not a script to complete regardless of where the customer is. When a customer reveals competitive context, the priority shifts to the already_saw_design reframe before returning to qualification.

quote: I never said you were. I'm just saying if you want help with that, I'll goodbye.
stewart_read: The rep abandons the call mid-sentence. There is no attempt to schedule a callback, leave a door open, or acknowledge LaFalla's stated preference one final time. A qualified, motivated customer — homeowner, bill pain, two competing quotes, actively seeking a third — exits the pipeline with no follow-up path. The mid-sentence hang-up is the most recoverable moment in the call: even a 'I'll send you an email with our contact info so you can reach us when you're ready' would have preserved the lead.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep recognized the call was unrecoverable and released cleanly to avoid further damage. Partially supported in principle, but the execution — abandoning mid-sentence — does not reflect a deliberate strategic release.
coaching_implication: Drill the graceful exit. Even when a call is unrecoverable, the rep's last line is a brand impression and a lead-preservation move. Suggested intent (not verbatim): acknowledge the preference, offer a concrete next step (email follow-up, callback at a time LaFalla chooses), and close warmly. A lead with two competing quotes who wants a third is worth preserving even if today's call is over.
